original_abstract:
We report the development of light-activated, single-component colloidal "dockers" capable of autonomous propulsion, cargo transport, and remote release. These micro-agents are synthesized from hematite, a photocatalytic iron oxide, which enables them to convert chemical energy from a hydrogen peroxide fuel into mechanical work upon exposure to visible light. The hematite particles—shaped like peanuts or cubes—possess a permanent magnetic moment, allowing for high-precision steering via a weak, uniform magnetic field. 

The propulsion and docking mechanisms are based on phoretic transport induced by chemical gradients at the particle interface. In basic solutions, activated dockers generate an attractive field that allows them to capture and tow passive colloidal cargo up to twenty times their own size. This docking behavior is entirely reversible; turning off the light source immediately halts propulsion and releases the cargo. Furthermore, the dockers exhibit a unique sensitivity to substrate topography, enabling them to follow pre-determined pathways along nanometer-scale tracks without external magnetic guidance. These versatile, bulk-synthesized particles offer a robust platform for micro-manufacturing and the creation of biomimetic systems, such as artificial microscopic muscles or robotic "shepherds" for directed assembly.
humanized_abstract:
Here, we demonstrate the creation of light-controlled, single-component colloidal “docker” particles that can self- propel, carry cargo and release it at a desired time and location. We made these micro-agents from hematite (iron oxide), a well-known photocatalyst and powered them in the presence of visible light to reduce hydrogen peroxide, providing the energy required to convert it into mechanical work. Our haematite “docker” particles are peanut or cubic shape with a permanent magnetic moment and can be controlled with great precision using a uniform magnetic field.

Phoretic propulsion and docking is demonstrated for particles composed of a core activated in basic solution surrounded by a layer of tethered chemiluminescent precursor and coated with poly-N-vinylpyrrolidone. In basic solution the core of the “activated dockers” generates a field that attracts colloidal cargo, which can be up to 20 times larger in size than the particle itself. The docking is a reversible process and the propulsion stops instantaneously when the light is switched off and the cargo is released. The particles can be directed along well-defined nanotracks on the substrate surface in a guided mode, without the need of an external permanent magnetic field. Potential applications of these particles are the development of micro-manufacturing technologies for bio-inspired systems and include for example artificial microscopic muscles or robotic “shepherds” that can assemble micro-particles.
